text: The esophagectomy, or esophagogastrectomy, is a surgical procedure performed for the treatment of esophageal cancer. During the procedure, we remove the bottom half of the esophagus and the top half of the stomach, as well as any surrounding lymph nodes. The remaining stomach is made into a tube and brought up into the chest and connected with the remaining esophagus. At TriHealth, we perform this surgery minimally invasively with the use of robotic technology. The following video is of a real esophagectomy procedure performed at TriHealth. The video has been edited and therefore is significantly shorter than a typical esophagectomy. The purpose of this video is to give patients an idea of how this surgery is performed.

Robotic instruments are visible as the surgeon lifts the stomach, exposing the omentum, a layer of fat connecting the stomach to the colon. The surgeon prepares to divide this layer while preserving the right gastroepiploic artery and vein, visible on the left side of the screen, which are crucial for maintaining blood supply to the newly formed gastric tube.

The surgeon works towards the spleen and diaphragm, dividing the layer of fat. The spleen is visible in the background, with the splenic artery seen at the bottom of the screen. The diaphragm appears as a muscular canopy over the spleen, extending to the left of the screen. The esophagus is separated from the cruce of the diaphragm, a muscular band wrapping around it at the esophageal hiatus.

The surgeon lifts the stomach and continues to divide the omentum, carefully preserving the right gastroepiploic artery and vein that run along the bottom portion of the stomach. The dissection progresses towards the pylorus, ensuring the preservation of the remaining blood supply necessary for the stomach's survival.

The surgeon uses a hook cautery device to carefully dissect and separate layers of fat surrounding the right gastroepiploic vein and artery, enhancing the mobility of the stomach for its repositioning into the chest. The wristed instruments provide dexterity for precise tissue division.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer, an energy device with a knife, to divide tissue while sealing blood vessels to prevent bleeding. The liver and gallbladder become visible, with the gallbladder appearing as a purple, bag-like structure.

The surgeon divides the fat layer to expose the duodenum, the first portion of the small intestine beyond the stomach. Tension is applied to the duodenum to facilitate tissue division, aiding in stomach mobilization for chest placement. The gallbladder is lifted, revealing the portohepatous region where the common bile duct is located. The duodenum is visualized behind other structures, attached to the pancreas and situated behind the right colon, necessitating separation of the right colon and associated mesentery from the duodenum.

The surgeon visually identifies the colon on the left side of the screen and the duodenum at the top right corner. The pancreas, appearing as yellowish peach-colored tissue, is attached to the duodenum. The surgeon continues to separate the right colon from the duodenum, a process known as cocorizing, to mobilize the duodenum for repositioning into the upper abdomen.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the duodenum by rolling it off the retroperitoneum, revealing the large purplish vena cava, a major vein transporting blood from the lower abdomen to the heart. The tissue over the vena cava is divided to allow greater mobility of the duodenum, facilitating the stomach's movement into the chest. Following this, a pyloromyotomy is performed, where the pylorus muscle at the stomach's end is divided to prevent contraction and ensure smooth passage of food into the small intestine.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to divide small muscle fibers near the pylorus to prevent blockage during food passage from the stomach. Following the completion of the pyloromyotomy, the surgeon proceeds with a lymphadenectomy, removing lymph nodes that drain the esophagus and stomach.

The surgeon lifts a lymph node near the hepatic artery and uses hook cautery to divide the connecting tissue. The hepatic artery lymph nodes are located above the hepatic artery, which will be skeletonized as the dissection progresses, revealing the artery clearly after removing the lymphatic tissue.

The surgeon identifies the coronary vein, also known as the left gastric vein, and proceeds to dissect around it, preparing to divide it at its base. The hepatic artery becomes visible as lymph nodes are lifted off of it, ensuring removal of all lymph nodes draining the esophagus and upper stomach. The coronary vein is traced to where it crosses behind the hepatic artery and inserts into the portal vein, while nodal tissue is meticulously removed for pathological examination.

Occasionally, we'll even see where there's no viable tumor left in the specimen once we take it out, and that's called a complete pathologic response. That's a very good sign if we see that. It's a good prognostic indicator, which means that the tumor itself is less likely to come back after surgery.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the coronary vein, revealing the left gastric artery behind it. The left gastric artery is then carefully dissected and divided.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to seal the left gastric artery. Following this, the lymphadenectomy proceeds, removing lymph node tissue from the area of the splenic artery up towards the cruce of the diaphragm, which is visible as a muscular structure.

The surgeon frees the remaining connective tissue around the esophagus, exposing the diaphragm. A hernia tape is wrapped around the esophagus and tied with a square knot, creating a handle for potential manipulation of the esophagus.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to create a gastric conduit by forming a tube from the stomach. The stapler, measuring six centimeters in length, is fired multiple times to construct the tube, which will serve as the new esophagus. The gastric blood vessel along the bottom edge of the stomach is preserved to ensure adequate blood supply to the conduit.

The surgeon has completed the creation of the gastric conduit by stapling across the stomach. A special camera is used to visualize the blood supply to the conduit after injecting a dye into the bloodstream, ensuring adequate perfusion.

The surgeon completes the gastric conduit creation and begins mediastinal dissection by pulling on the hernia tape wrapped around the esophagus, creating tension for dissection. The native esophagus is visible at the top of the screen as a tube. The procedure transitions from the abdomen to the chest area, specifically the mediastinum, which is located between the right and left lungs and contains the esophagus, heart, and aorta. The diaphragm is lifted, revealing a black nodule identified as a lymph node.

The surgeon removes a lymph node for cancer evaluation and continues dissection into the chest cavity. The esophagus is lifted off the aorta, visible at the bottom of the screen, and is fully mobilized from the abdomen. The esophagus is then divided, creating a free specimen, which is removed from the abdomen for pathological examination of the margins.

The surgeon pulls the gastric conduit, referred to as the new esophagus, up into the chest cavity, ensuring it remains untwisted and properly oriented by securing it with sutures to a drain. The specimen is placed in a retrieval pouch for removal and pathology examination. The patient is repositioned on their left side, with the right side facing up. The cut edge of the esophagus with the staple line and attached drain is visible, and the surgeon continues to mobilize the esophagus towards the upper chest for anastomosis, with the azagus vein running alongside it.

The surgeon identifies the azagus vein crossing over the esophagus and uses a stapler to divide it, creating a window for further dissection. This step is crucial to ensure an adequate margin and facilitate a long anastomosis that will prevent narrowing or stricture post-surgery.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the esophagus, pulling the stomach conduit up into the chest along with a previously placed drain. The stomach conduit reaches the top portion of the chest, ensuring adequate length for anastomosis. A hole is made in the stomach conduit and another in the esophagus, and a stapler is used to create a large opening between them. A nasogastric tube is inserted through the nose, running down the esophagus into the stomach, to aid healing and prevent leaks post-surgery.

The surgeon uses sutures to close the common opening left after stapling, employing robotic instruments through small incisions between the ribs to achieve precise movements. This minimally invasive approach contrasts with traditional large thoracotomy incisions, reducing postoperative pain and facilitating faster recovery.

The surgeon completes the first layer of the esophagogastric anastomosis by tying down the sutures, then begins the second layer using interrupted sutures to further secure the connection between the esophagus and stomach. The completed anastomosis is irrigated and cleaned, and the drain is positioned correctly within the chest cavity, concluding the minimally invasive transthoracic esophagogastrectomy.
